course_description: The speculative short story is one of the most provocative and intriguing narrative forms available to writers and readers: it hits like a gut punch; it should astonish, mystify, amaze, wound and console—all in the space of a few pages. In this course, you will develop the skills necessary to write compelling and thought-provoking speculative short stories. Through readings, discussions, and writing exercises, you will explore the various sub-genres of speculative fiction, including science fiction, fantasy, horror, and magical realism. Topics covered may include discovering and developing prompts, narrative structure, worldbuilding, and thematic elements. You will also analyze important works of speculative fiction and their publishing contexts to gain a better understanding of the genre's conventions, techniques and markets. By the end of the course, you will have written and received feedback on a speculative short story of your own and prepared it for submission.

restrictions: Course offering may be cancelled unless a minimum of 10 students enrol.

with:

A list of three tutorial preferences (in order of preference)
Reason for the change – e.g. timetable clash, elite athlete status, SAP
Teaching staff do not have access to change tutorials or help with timetables; all timetabling changes must be processed through the Timetabling Team.

learning_outcomes.description: Analyse and critique examples of speculative short fiction for form, genre, and technique
Write a complete speculative short story using knowledge about the form, skills and technique
Understand the market for speculative short stories and how to pursue publication

assessment_details.title: Critical Commentary
assessment_details.task_description: Objective: This reflective assessment aims to explore your personal experience and insights gained from a specific creative writing lesson and exercise. Through this assignment, you'll reflect on your writing and learning practice and consider how it will impact your future writing endeavors. Instructions: Select a creative writing lesson and exercise from those we have completed in class. Reflect on your personal experience with the lesson and exercise: Describe what you attempted during the exercise and why. Share any challenges you faced and any successes you achieved. Analyze what you learned: Discuss how your understanding of creative writing has evolved. Highlight any insights or new perspectives gained. Plan for future application: Explain how you intend to incorporate what you've learned into your writing practice. Please note that reflective essays can still make use of secondary sources–craft manuals, author interviews, essays, articles, and others, though they do not have to. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using TurnItIn . Before submitting any assignments for this course you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
When you successfully submit your assessment to TurnItIn you will see text confirming your submission is complete, before being redirected to your Assignment inbox. On this page you can:
View the name of the submitted file View date and time of the upload Resubmit your paper (if necessary) Download your submitted paper Download digital receipt.
If you cannot see your submission in your Assignment inbox you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your TurnItIn assignment .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Workshopping and Seminar Engagement
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment asks students to critically engage with the course by submitting your own work for feedback and for providing structured feedback on the stories submitted by your classmates. You will provide a record of your feedback and a brief reflection on your engagement in the course. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. assessment_details.title: Short Story
assessment_details.due_date: 31/10/2025 4:00 pm Please submit in standard manuscript format.
assessment_details.task_description: Students will submit an edited speculative short story in standard manuscript format. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. assessment_details.title: Draft Submission and Presentation
assessment_details.due_date: 8/09/2025 - 6/10/2025 The submission date of the draft short story will be determined via a sign-up sheet at the start of class. The student will be required to present in class. If they are unable to do so, they should consult the Course Coordinator. In the first instance, the Coordinator will try to reschedule the presentation. If this is not possible, the student must attend an interview with the Course Coordinator to verify authorship of the draft, which will be recorded. Passing this interview constitutes a PASS for the assessment. See " EXTENSION OR DEFERRAL AVAILABILITY NOTES"
assessment_details.task_description: This secure assessment consists of two parts: Submit a draft of your short story for workshopping (see the above assessment) and then lead a small group discussion about the feedback you have received Complete an in-class reflection via Blackboard during the final 20 minutes of your workshop session This pass/fail hurdle requirement verifies student authorship of creative work and engagement with the workshop process. To pass the course, students must successfully complete both components of this secure assessment. Component 1: Workshop Participation To achieve a PASS, you must: Attend the full workshop session OR complete an approved supervised make-up assessment Demonstrate engagement with peer feedback on your work Show evidence of authorship through discussion of your creative choices Component 2: In-Class Workshop Reflection To achieve a PASS, you must submit a reflection that: Responds to the prompts about the feedback you received Demonstrates understanding of your own creative work Shows thoughtful consideration of revision plans Is completed and submitted via Blackboard during the workshop session Assessment Outcomes PASS Students who meet all criteria for both components will receive a PASS for this hurdle requirement and may continue in the course. FAIL Students who do not meet the criteria for either component will receive a FAIL for this hurdle requirement, resulting in a grade of 1 for the entire course, regardless of performance in other assessment items. If you are at risk of failing the hurdle requirement, you will be invited to a follow-up interview to verify authorship and discuss your work. A PASS during this interview will constitute a PASS for both components. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit via Blackboard (or via a paper form in case of technical difficulties)
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
As this is a scheduled in-class activity and requires the participation of other students, students who cannot submit on time and/or attend due to serious illness or exceptional circumstances should:
Notify the instructor before the scheduled workshop where possible Provide appropriate documentation
Depending on the circumstances, the Course Coordinator may:
allow late submission; re-schedule your workshop week; arrange an interview to verify authorship of the draft.
Students who fail to submit on time or to attend the workshopping session may not receive the benefit of feedback on their draft.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Note, this is a pass/fail hurdle. It is not graded. See the notes under "Other Due Information" for what to do if you cannot attend your presentation date.

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: WEEK 1: A World in Miniature: Introduction to the Speculative Short Story Introduction to assessments; discussion of the nature of the short story; close reading and discussion of "The Santa Clause Parade" by Helen Marshall. Learning outcomes: L01, L02

WEEK 2: The Fundamentals of Speculation: Premises, Development and Worldbuilding Discussion of coming up with premises for short stories, how to develop them within the limitations of a tight word count, analysis of assigned short stories. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

WEEK 3: Words that Cut Like Glass: Playing with Form and Language Discussion of the language of short story, how it links to form, creative exercises, analysis of assigned short stories. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

WEEK 4: In Play and Replay: Experimentations with Genre Discussion of use of genres, their limitations and affordances, creative exercises, analysis of assigned short stories. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

WEEK 5: Happily Ever After?: Ending on a High Note Discussion of short story endings and their emotional effects, creative exercises, analysis of assigned short stories. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
WEEK 6: NO CLASS Work on your assessment
WEEK 7: Workshopping Workshopping of short stories Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
WEEK 8: Workshopping Workshopping of short stories Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
WEEK 9: Workshopping Workshopping of short stories Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
WEEK 10: Workshopping Workshopping of short stories Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

WEEK 11: The Devil in the Details: Editing and Publishing Speculative Short Stories Discussion of editing practices and making sense of feedback, discussion of the market. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Course guidelines Communication Expectations While you are a student at UQ, all communication must be conducted according to the UQ Student Code of Conduct . The UQ Library has a helpful Communicate and collaborate online module . Email is the primary way for you to send messages to, and receive information from, the School and our staff. You must use your UQ email address (not a private address) to communicate with staff. You should add a clear subject line, including course code, and a 2-3 word statement. You can send email at any time, however, please do not expect responses outside normal working hours (Monday to Friday from ~8am to ~5pm). Emails that constitute bullying, harassment or discrimination against staff contravene the Student Code of Conduct. Emails like this will be reported to the University, and the matter will be pursued as misconduct. Learning activities
